data.title: Malaysia’s Umno chief denies he ‘sidelined’ Najib to secure his own legal freedom
data.description:
The president of Malaysia’s Umno has denied abandoning disgraced ex-prime minister Najib Razak to save himself, as fissures emerge in the former ruling party over the mixed legal fortunes of two of its top leaders.
Umno grass-roots members have urged President Ahmad Zahid Hamidi to quit Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim’s government in protest after the High Court blocked Najib’s bid to serve the remainder of his jail term under house arrest last month. It also handed the one-time party figurehead a fresh 15-year jail sentence for corruption linked to state fund 1MDB.
In contrast, the attorney general last week cleared Ahmad Zahid of corruption charges linked to a charitable body that he founded, saying there were insufficient grounds to pursue the case further.
Umno, which led the country for six decades, in 2022 joined Anwar’s rag-tag coalition of political friends and foes bound together at the behest of then king Sultan Abdullah Ahmad Shah.

On Friday, Ahmad Zahid dismissed claims that he had left Najib to his fate.
“There has been talk that the decision to join the coalition government was for Zahid to save himself. That is the accusation,” the deputy prime minister said in his policy speech at Umno’s general assembly.
“There are claims that Zahid had sidelined his friend Najib … Umno has never wavered in its position to stand with Datuk Seri Najib,” he said, using Najib’s honorific.
Pro-Najib factions in Umno have urged the party leadership to leave the government and revisit its pact with the Pan-Malaysian Islamic Party, potentially creating a formidable coalition representing the country’s nearly 20 million Malay Muslims.
Najib, 72, is currently serving a six-year reduced sentence after receiving partial royal clemency for stealing 42 million ringgit (US$10.3 million) from SRC International, a former unit of 1MDB.
But he now faces an additional 15-year jail term and 11.4 billion ringgit fine after the High Court convicted him of 25 counts of money laundering and abuse of power linked to the 1MDB sovereign wealth fund on December 26.


Najib established the fund, which saw billions plundered from Malaysian taxpayers.
Ahmad Zahid said Umno’s leadership was aware of the grass-roots disappointment but urged for patience as the party appealed to current king Sultan Ibrahim Iskandar to allow Najib “to return to his family and be with us all”.
He reaffirmed the party’s commitment to stay with Anwar’s administration for the remainder of his term – which ends in two years – but cautioned coalition partners against crossing the party’s red lines.
Umno and erstwhile rival Democratic Action Party (DAP), a member of Anwar’s Pakatan Harapan alliance, have butted heads in recent weeks after leaders of the Chinese-dominated party celebrated the court’s rulings against Najib.
Though it has 26 parliamentary seats to the DAP’s 40, Umno serves as an important bridge for Anwar’s outreach to the country’s Malay-Muslim majority.
“We will not take this sitting down. Political cooperation must be built on trust, integrity and respect,” Ahmad Zahid said.

data.title: Trump pick for South America command sidesteps China questions at Senate hearing
data.description:
Days after the operation that abducted then Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro, President Donald Trump’s nominee to command US forces in Latin America dodged pressure from senators to endorse a military-first plan to counter China’s growing influence in the region.
Lieutenant General Francis Donovan, who is nominated to lead US Southern Command (Southcom), repeatedly declined to say whether Washington should rely mainly on force projection to push back against Beijing’s expanding footprint.
The evasiveness shaped much of his confirmation hearing before the Senate Armed Services Committee, where lawmakers sought clarity on how Southcom would operate under the new strategic environment created by the Maduro raid.
Donovan’s nomination comes amid heightened strategic tension in the region, with the Maduro operation energising Trump’s political allies, unsettling regional capitals and leaving a substantial US military presence in the Caribbean.
China and Russia’s deepening economic, technological and defence engagement across South America prompted senators to frame the hearing as a test of how Southcom intended to navigate both a transformed security landscape and intensifying great power competition.


In response to questions about both Beijing and Moscow, Donovan acknowledged the need to balance instruments of influence, saying the US needed to “rely more on soft aspects of power, complemented when required by hard military power to counter those threats”.
A career special operator with the US Marine Corps, Donovan has served in clandestine and high-intensity combat operations, commanded marine and joint forces in the Middle East and most recently served as vice-commander of US Special Operations Command.
Donovan had been widely expected in Washington defence circles to reflect a preference for kinetic expertise inside a command historically defined by security cooperation and inter-agency coordination.
If confirmed, he will replace Admiral Alvin Holsey, who resigned abruptly last year after barely 12 months in the role, a departure that reshaped expectations around Southcom and gave Donovan’s nomination a political weight beyond routine personnel change.
Holsey had championed a diplomatic and inter-agency approach to China that stressed integrated deterrence, intelligence sharing with regional governments and coordination with civilian agencies to expose Beijing’s activities.
His emphasis on soft power instruments and inter-agency tools reflected a long-standing Southcom tradition of engagement rather than confrontation.
Holsey’s exit followed disagreements with the administration over lethal maritime strikes on suspected narcotrafficking vessels, a dispute that highlighted a widening gap between his style and the White House’s preference for more forceful measures.
Inside the Pentagon, the episode was read as evidence that Trump wanted more assertive leadership at Southcom, with the administration positioning Donovan to reinforce that shift in a broader pattern of elevating special operations officers and advocating a tougher approach to near-peer rivals in the Americas.
Even so, none of that emerged in Donovan’s testimony, where he instead counselled caution and process.




Donovan said he lacked access to the intelligence needed to assess Southcom’s assets for a China-focused mission, was unsure how long the naval build-up in the Caribbean was meant to persist and could not judge the state of inter-agency coordination without first taking command.
Lieutenant General Joshua Rudd, who was appearing alongside Donovan as Trump’s nominee to lead US Cyber Command, adopted the same stance.
Both men stressed that they were not yet in their intended posts and therefore could not critique existing plans or resources. The combined evasiveness irritated members of both parties, including Republicans supportive of Trump’s regional ambitions.
“It is OK for you two gentlemen to tell us how you feel,” said committee chairman Roger Wicker, a Republican from Mississippi, expressing frustration.
“We realise that you have not been confirmed yet, but so far we really have not received any answers about how you feel about the responsibility you are about to take on. It is OK to tell us and actually to be helpful.”
Much of that frustration centred on China, which Wicker framed in his opening statement as a strategic threat to the United States.
Citing Chinese control of telecommunications systems, ports and critical infrastructure, he warned that China used large commodity deals to deepen economic dependence and reduce US access.
“Most troubling, China has expanded defence cooperation with arms sales, police training and space and cyber partnerships, all to the detriment of our country,” Wicker said.


Ranking member Jack Reed, a Democrat from Rhode Island, raised related concerns, noting that Southcom had long faced resource constraints while grappling with near-peer rivals.
Reed highlighted Chinese investments in what he called “strategic locations throughout South America” and warned that the recent surge of US forces in the theatre “does not seem to be postured for threats from near-peer competitors”.
Senator Rick Scott, a Republican from Florida – who praised the Maduro operation as “flawless” – was more direct, asking how “dictators like Xi and Putin” might have viewed it.
Donovan responded that he had no intelligence reporting on reactions in Beijing or Moscow but hoped they were paying attention.
“I have to believe they would be very wary of the US military. The precision execution at range, speed and scale, it was phenomenal. It is what we bring to the fight across the globe,” he added.
Senator Richard Blumenthal, a Democrat from Connecticut, pressed Donovan on how he viewed China and Russia as threats inside Southcom’s area of responsibility. Donovan responded that he would dive deep into the issue if confirmed.
“I believe that both of those actors would be wary of military competition because they would be extended from their source of strength and power,” he said.

Donovan also noted that China and Russia were already “well ingrained throughout the Southcom theatre” and that most of the response or countermeasures would come from the economic side, using other levers to account for those intrusions.
The hearing unfolded as the administration was redefining US strategy for the Americas.
In December, the White House released a new national security strategy that designated the western hemisphere as a core security zone for the United States and pledged to prevent outside powers from controlling critical infrastructure in the region.
In US military parlance, the western hemisphere refers to the Americas as a geopolitical buffer tied directly to homeland defence, reflecting concepts rooted in the 19th-century Monroe Doctrine that warned European powers against meddling in the newly independent nations of the Americas.
The new strategy commits Washington to denying “non-hemispheric competitors” the ability to position forces or acquire critical infrastructure in the region, placing it at the centre of US national security rather than as a theatre for diplomacy alone.

data.title: Number of registered companies in Hong Kong reaches record 1.56 million in 2025
data.description:
Hong Kong’s corporate landscape expanded to unprecedented levels last year, with the total number of local registered companies reaching a record of nearly 1.56 million by the end of 2025.
“By the end of 2025, the total number of local companies and redomiciled companies registered under the Companies Ordinance increased by 96,609 compared with 2024, reaching 1,557,103,” the Companies Registry said in a statement on Friday, noting that it was “an all-time high figure”.
Among the total in 2025, 195,343 were newly registered local and redomiciled firms, while 99,406 were dissolved over the same period.
The increase in local firms occurred alongside a significant rise in non-Hong Kong companies establishing a place of business in the city. By the end of 2025, the number of such firms stood at 15,586, up 3 per cent from 2024, with 1,532 newcomers registered during the year.
A key driver of this momentum was the company redomiciliation regime, implemented on May 23 last year, which allows non-Hong Kong incorporated companies to relocate their registration to the city.
“The company redomiciliation regime … has received a positive market response,” the registry said. “It ensures seamless business continuity and strengthens Hong Kong’s competitiveness as a global business and financial hub.”
By the end of 2025, the registry received over 420 inquiries and 30 applications regarding the new mechanism.
Among these, six non-Hong Kong corporations incorporated in Luxembourg, the Cayman Islands or Bermuda, including an insurance company, were successfully redomiciled to Hong Kong.
While the registry did not name specific entities, the past year also saw high-profile moves from major conglomerates.
State-owned China Resources Group saw two of its listed units, China Resources Beverage and China Resources Building Materials Technology, propose reincorporating from the Cayman Islands to Hong Kong, to reduce compliance costs and align with their business focus.
Similarly, insurers such as AXA and Manulife announced plans to shift their domicile from Bermuda to Hong Kong, with PetroChina Investment (Hong Kong) becoming the first to complete the process in October.
The registry said the regime allowed companies to relocate “by eliminating the need for complicated and costly judicial procedures while preserving a company’s legal identity”.

Beyond corporate registrations, the financial sector recorded strong growth in fund structures. The total number of open-ended fund companies (OFCs) surged 43.2 per cent to 676 by the end of 2025, following 210 new incorporations during the year.
The main purpose of an OFC is to serve as an investment fund vehicle and manage investments for the benefit of its shareholders.
Limited partnership funds (LPFs), another type of investment vehicle, also saw a double-digit increase, rising by 35.1 per cent to 1,347 after 389 new funds were registered.
Operational activity at the registry reflected this expansion. The number of documents delivered for registration rose by 7 per cent to 3,360,723, while electronic searches of document image records increased by 0.7 per cent to over 5.22 million.
In the lending and trust sectors, the registry granted 760 new licences to trust or company service providers, bringing the total to 7,220.
In addition, the Licensing Court granted 155 new money lenders’ licences, with the total number of licensed lenders reaching 2,015 by year end.

data.title: China ‘hardcore grandma’ gains fame for farm fertilisation drone skills and as live-streamer
data.description:
An 82-year-old woman from eastern China, affectionately known as the “hardcore grandma,” has captivated many with her remarkable skills in operating field fertilisation drones and her success as a live-streamer selling rice.
Born in 1943, Dai Shuying hails from Laomei Village in Xindu Town, Tongcheng, Anhui province.
In her youth, she worked as a bookkeeper and was regarded as one of the few “educated individuals” in her village, capable of reading, writing, and performing calculations.
While raising five children – three of whom eventually moved to cities – Dai remained in the village with her eldest son and her 40-year-old grandson, Wang Tiantian. Together, they manage over 600 mu (40 hectares) of farmland.

Although she has stepped back from physically demanding farm work, Dai maintains her energy and good health.
Her journey into drone technology began out of curiosity when her grandson brought home a large agricultural drone to help manage their farmland.
Intrigued by the imposing “iron lump”, Dai asked Wang to teach her how to operate it.
With her grandson serving as her flight instructor, she quickly learned to install batteries weighing 15 kilos and perform essential operations.

As she gained hands-on experience, Dai became skilled at transporting the drone, unfolding its wings, adding fertiliser, and even personally managing take-offs while executing fertilisation tasks with precision.
The transition from manual labour to drone technology has significantly enhanced the family’s agricultural output.
“Back then, one person could only treat just over 10 mu (0.67 hectare) in an entire day,” Dai explained. “With this drone, we can finish all 600 mu in one day. It saves time and effort.”
Wang created a short video account called “Old Farmer, Gratefully Loved” to document their daily life.

The account features his grandmother flying drones and their humorous interactions, attracting over 210,000 followers on Douyin.
To supplement the family’s income, the duo later launched a live-streaming channel, promoting and selling their home-grown rice, cooking oil, and other agricultural products.
Dai can even confidently operate live-streaming equipment on her own.
“Grandma learns to read and write quickly, but the most important thing is patience,” Wang shared when asked about his secret to teaching seniors.

Dai views her new career as both entertainment and a way to stay close to her family. She embraces new experiences and does not perceive herself as old.
“Life is very happy now. My children are by my side, I enjoy farming, and our income is better than before. At over 80 years of age, being able to see so much of the world is the best part,” she said.
Her story has sparked an outpouring of admiration on social media, with netizens affectionately dubbing her a “hardcore grandma.”
One person commented: “I absolutely love this grandma. She can do everything!”
Another added: “Wishing grandma good health and a long life!”

data.title: Meet Loli Bahia, the model who’s BFFs with Alex Consani and rumoured to be dating Rosalía
data.description:
New year, new celebrity couple. Spanish singer Rosalía, 33, has been cosying up to French model Loli Bahia, who’s 10 years her junior. From holidaying in Rio de Janeiro to looking loved up on the streets of Madrid and, most recently, going on a Parisian shopping spree, the jet-setting pair seem to be growing closer than ever.

The “Berghain” singer, who received critical acclaim for her 2025 record Lux, is about to embark on a world tour for the album in March this year. Bahia reportedly attended her album launch in Madrid, and they apparently enjoyed a Lady Gaga concert in Paris, too.
Rosalía was previously linked to German actor-musician Emilio Sakraya, The Bear star Jeremy Allen White, musician Rauw Alejandro, Euphoria actress Hunter Schafer and rapper C. Tangana.

So, who is Loli Bahia, who counts Alex Consani, Rachel Marx, Vittoria Ceretti and Lulu Tenney as BFFs?
What’s her background?

Bahia hails from the French city of Lyon, which is where Rosalía will be opening her Lux tour. Her father is reportedly Spanish and her mother was born in Algeria. She grew up in La Croix-Rousse, per Vogue Arabia, and spent part of her childhood in Algeria and Andalusia, with family in Oran, Béni Saf and southern Spain.
She’s a model and actress

According to Vogue Arabia, Bahia caught the eye of a scout when she was tagging along with a friend to a modelling competition. At 17, she made her modelling debut, walking for Louis Vuitton. Since then, she has worked with brands like Chanel, Dior, Prada and more. “It took me about three and a half years to really learn how to enjoy [being a model],” she told Vogue Arabia, adding, “Fashion has really helped me feel comfortable as a woman, and be comfortable moving around.”
Bahia made her acting debut in 2023’s Jeanne du Barry, playing a younger version of the titular character. She’s now developing her acting skills and even wants to play James Bond one day. “I could never be a Bond girl. They’re always still standing behind the man,” she explained.
She almost didn’t become a model












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by Loli bahia (@lolibahiaa)



In her early years, Bahia pursued music and sports, per Vogue Arabia. Her journey as a classical instrumentalist started when she was five. She went on to study music at a conservatory.
The fashion star was also involved in judo, karate, swimming and gymnastics, played football and basketball, and was a competitive volleyball player for a decade. “I stopped [playing sports] when I started modelling because I couldn’t commit to a team, but if I didn’t model, I would’ve done it professionally. In my wildest dreams, I would’ve been an Olympian,” she told Vogue Arabia.

data.title: HSBC reviews Singapore insurance unit amid focus on high growth in Hong Kong, China
data.description:
HSBC Holdings’ exploration of options for its insurance business in Singapore may lead to a sale of the unit as the group reallocates resources to its insurance business in Hong Kong and mainland China, according to analysts.
“It makes sense for HSBC to consider an exit from Singapore’s insurance market to focus on Hong Kong and mainland China, as so many mainlanders are coming to Hong Kong to buy insurance products here,” said Kenny Tang Sing-hing, chairman of the Hong Kong Institute of Financial Analysts and Professional Commentators.
“The review will only cover HSBC Life Singapore and will consider all options for the insurance manufacturing business, with no decision made,” HSBC said in a statement on Friday.
The revelation of the review came just a week after HSBC succeeded in its bid to buy out the remaining 37 per cent of Hang Seng Bank that it did not already own. On January 8, Hang Seng Bank shareholders approved the lender’s privatisation by the parent company at a total cost of HK$106.6 billion (US$13.6 billion).
“The review is part of the group’s ongoing simplification globally,” the bank’s statement said. “HSBC is focused on increasing leadership and market share in the areas where it has a clear competitive advantage and where it has the greatest opportunities to grow and support its clients.”
HSBC CEO Georges Elhedery in February last year unveiled his plan to redeploy US$1.5 billion from “low-return” areas to high-growth ­businesses in Hong Kong, mainland China, India and other Asian markets.
In the third quarter, it exited Sri Lanka retail banking and Malta. Earlier last year, it sold its private bank in Germany and an insurance business in France, and decided to exit investment banking in Europe and the US.
Globally, insurance is a growth engine for HSBC, contributing over a quarter of its wealth-management revenue in the first half of last year. The group’s HSBC Life is the biggest insurance provider in Hong Kong in terms of new sales over the last three years, with a market share of 27 per cent in the first half of 2025.

“Hong Kong and mainland China are seen as core high-growth markets for wealth creation, intergenerational transfers and underpenetrated insurance needs,” said Tom Chan Pak-lam, permanent honorary president of the Institute of Securities Dealers, adding that the Singapore unit was not as dominant.
“Under Elhedery, the bank is reallocating resources away from areas that don’t deliver the highest returns or strategic fit,” Chan said. “Selling a noncore insurance manufacturing arm could free up capital to double down on Hong Kong and China growth, wealth distribution or other high-potential areas.”
HSBC said it still considered Singapore “a priority market” where it continued to “accelerate growth” in wealth and wholesale banking and would continue to “offer market-leading insurance products”.
Chan said this statement suggested the group could opt to stop underwriting policies in Singapore and instead sell policies underwritten by other insurers.
Singapore was HSBC’s fifth largest profit contributor in 2024, with US$1.4 billion in profit before tax. HSBC served more than 4,000 customers in the city and had continued to invest in digital banking and its physical network in Singapore over the past five years, the lender said.

data.title: Alibaba’s upgraded Qwen chatbot app receives warm consumer reception
data.description:
Alibaba’s ambition to turn its chatbot app into a powerful AI assistant that dramatically changes the way people interact with smart devices appears to be paying off, according to users and analysts.
Instead of repeatedly swiping a screen to complete a task, a user can tell the Qwen app to conduct a range of tasks from ordering drinks to paying electricity bills after the Hangzhou-based technology giant links it to the group’s existing apps like shopping platform Taobao and payment interface Alipay. The app has been developed using Alibaba’s Qwen family of large language models. Alibaba owns the Post.
Consumers hailed the change, saying the Qwen app could be the gateway to access Alibaba’s AI capabilities and varied services, liberating users from time-consuming operations such as comparing prices from different vendors. With the switch, Qwen has upgraded from a chatbot into a virtual “life assistant” for hundreds of millions of consumers.
“I tried to order meals using Qwen and it went smoothly,” said Yang Tao, who works in the new-energy vehicle sector in Nanjing, on Thursday. It would be particularly useful for senior citizens who might not be good at navigating different apps, he added.

The updated Qwen app was launched on Thursday, with Wu Jia, Alibaba’s vice-president in charge of consumer-facing AI, showing its capabilities by ordering 40 cups of milk tea to the site of the launch ceremony.
The upgraded Qwen app represents Alibaba’s latest effort to create user-friendly interfaces by leveraging its powerful underlying AI agentic capabilities. Besides Taobao and Alipay, the app also supports the on-demand delivery platform Taobao Shangou, online travel agency Fliggy and mapping service Amap – all part of the group’s extensive consumer services ecosystem.
Zhuang Shuai, founder and chief analyst at e-commerce consultancy Bailian, said Alibaba’s updated Qwen app could change the online shopping ecosystem.
“Going forward e-commerce won’t just be about competing on clicks or price,” Zhuang said, adding that it would be decided by whether a site has enough products and services that can be analysed and recommended by AI.
Morgan Stanley said in a recent note that Qwen was “well positioned to become an all-in-one AI super-app and life assistant”.
A partnership Google and Walmart has made it easier for users to discover and buy items from the US supermarket chain using Gemini, Google’s AI assistant, but Alibaba’s Qwen app offers a wide range of services that go beyond online shopping.
Alibaba listed 400 day-to-day tasks that can be handled by the Qwen app, including booking tables at restaurants and tracking price changes.
A test by a Post reporter showed that the app was able to understand oral instructions and provide information when asked to list newly opened bubble tea shops in the Galleria Mall in Beijing.

data.title: World Snooker Grand Prix will have Kai Tak home until 2029, but will O’Sullivan ever come?
data.description:
Snooker in Hong Kong will have a home at Kai Tak Arena for the next four years, organisers of the World Grand Prix said on Friday, although whether Ronnie O’Sullivan makes an appearance is still in doubt.
The mercurial Englishman skipped last year’s tournament in the city, and although he is on the list of the top 32 players expected to compete at Hong Kong’s newest venue, he has withdrawn from this week’s Masters on medical grounds.
Still, the likes of defending champion Neil Robertson, reigning world champion Zhao Xintong, Judd Trump, Mark Selby and 10 Chinese professionals will make the trip, giving local fans something to get excited about.
The organisers also revealed some changes to the set-up and that they wanted to align future ticket sales of the event with the usual practice of advanced sales adopted by the other World Snooker Tour events in the United Kingdom.
“We redesigned the venue and doubled the number of tables in the earlier rounds, and the process, including any discussions [with relevant parties], was lengthy,” Lakita Feng Huanhuan, CEO of organisers F-Sports, said.

“I’d rather not go through it from scratch again, but of course, there could be some fine-tuning and it depends on the viewing experience of the spectators.
“If the new settings were good, we could also follow the UK practice to advance the ticket sales for the next year, immediately after the preceding event.
“Our target is to implement this for the 2028 and 2029 events because there have been voices in the UK asking about the feasibility of such advanced sale … the World Grand Prix is not an event just for the local fans but every snooker fanatic from around the world.”
Among the 10 Chinese players competing in the city next month was Zhou Yuelong, who will be playing in Hong Kong for the first time.
“I had played professionally for many years, but I had never played here, so I hope to perform well here,” the world No 16 from Sichuan said. “I’ll do the best I can and try to achieve the best results.
“With the format now changed to the best-of-nine frames, I think this could be a change that benefits those players who didn’t start the match well. As for the spectators, they could also watch their favourite players longer, so I believe this is going to be good.”
Zhou also said the large number of Chinese players at the World Grand Prix, which runs from February 3 to 8, marked the growth of the sport in China, and he hoped to see himself, or a compatriot, triumphing in Hong Kong next month.
Local snooker star Marco Fu Ka-chun, the former world No 5 and a winner of three ranking events, was set to feature in two exhibition matches against seven-time world champion Stephen Hendry.
“I had practised with him for a long period at the start of my career, which laid a good foundation for me in terms of both my skills and mindset,” the 48-year-old said. “So being able to play against Stephen at this stage of my career was meaningful.
“It’s like paying a tribute to my idol on home soil; I am honoured and will be looking forward to it.”
Currently ranked No 75 in the world, Fu said that he was approaching the end of his professional career and admitted “it’s going to be very difficult for me to return to my previous peak. This is the reality for any athlete, not just me”.

data.title: Why is China’s Xi Jinping putting the political heat back on illegal villas and resorts?
data.description:
Chinese President Xi Jinping has refocused national anti-corruption efforts on illegal villas and “private resorts”, luxury developments that have come to symbolise excess and graft over the past two decades.
According to newly released excerpts of a speech, Xi singled out the developments for special mention at the central urban work conference in July, highlighting them as examples of the need to improve governance, combat corruption and promote sustainable urban development.
“[Officials must] conserve and efficiently utilise resources, strengthen regulation and absolutely prohibit illegal and irregular construction of luxury villas and private resorts,” he said, according to the excerpts published on Thursday in Qiushi, the Communist Party’s leading theoretical journal.
Xi said the country had made remarkable urban progress over the years, including extensive infrastructure upgrades, expanded city clusters and improved public services.
However, some officials and wealthy individuals have exploited loopholes to build luxury villas and private estates, often occupying large areas of land, damaging the environment and flouting urban planning standards.


Xi has made eradicating these kinds of luxury developments a regular theme over the years, issuing calls both on environmental and social equity grounds.
The calls align with the national goals of reducing income disparity, promoting green and sustainable development, and encouraging modest living standards as the country transitions from rapid growth to stable, quality-driven development.
In 2020, during a trip to the northwestern province of Shaanxi, Xi highlighted the case of illegal villas built in a nature reserve at the northern foot of the Qinling mountain range.
Work started on the villas around 2000 and involved long-term illegal land occupation and severe damage to the environment.
Despite multiple directives from Xi from 2014 to 2018 to demolish these villas and rehabilitate the area, local officials repeatedly ignored or delayed action, according to state media reports.
In July 2018, after Xi issued his sixth instruction on the matter, the central authorities dispatched a working group to Shaanxi to investigate. The group identified more than 1,100 illegally built villas, which were swiftly demolished and the land returned to the reserve.
In the political storm, hundreds of Shaanxi officials were investigated for corruption and dereliction of duty, including the province’s former Communist Party boss, Zhao Zhengyong, who was given a suspended death sentence.
The Qinling case has since been cited as a warning to officials who ignore the orders from the central government, with a series of crackdowns on similar luxury projects since then.


In 2021, authorities in Kunming, capital of the southwestern province of Yunnan, identified more than 1,000 villas and flats illegally built along the banks of Dianchi Lake, a vital environmental and scenic area.
Despite prior orders from Beijing in 2016 to halt and demolish these developments, local officials delayed enforcement, allowing extensive illegal construction.
The authorities finally acted after the case was singled out in a report by inspectors from the Ministry of Ecology and Environment – one of the most high-profile criticisms from the central government.
Local officials then sent in the bulldozers to level 813 villas and 294 flats covering 230 hectares (568 acres) and 1,700 workers to plant saplings to help repair the degraded landscape, the official Kunming Daily reported at the time.
Environmental protection has become an increasingly important criterion in the performance assessments of local officials.
Each year, China conducts regular environmental inspections, dispatching multiple inspection teams led by ministerial-level officials to local governments to review environmental efforts and ensure compliance.

data.title: Canada to remove 100% tariffs on Chinese EV imports: prime minister
data.description:
Canada will remove the additional 100 per cent in tariffs it had imposed on Chinese electric vehicle (EV) imports, Prime Minister Mark Carney said during a state visit to Beijing.
In place of the higher duties, Carney said, Canada will cap import volume and apply a standard tariff rate of 6.1 per cent to shipments within the 49,000-unit limit.
More to follow...
